q3: The middle section kept losing the thread between naming the problem and landing the solution — it would do good rhetorical work but skip the explanatory sentence that made the benefit feel earned rather than claimed. It took a few rounds to get that bridge in place.
q5: Honestly, yes — the rubric was pretty well-aligned with what I actually care about. The stuff about tension in headlines, integrated social proof, and CTAs feeling like a natural exhale? That's exactly how I think about pages. It wasn't a perfect translation — 'quiet confidence' is a little vague as a criterion — but it was closer to my real preferences than I expected a rubric to get.
q6: What it surfaced for me was that the model was consistently stronger on tone and voice than on the structural stuff — the benefit-grounding and the social proof integration. When the rubric flagged those explicitly, the model's responses got more deliberate about them. Without it I might have just felt like something was off without being able to name what.

q3: The social proof placement kept defaulting to a bolted-on quote at the end — it took a round of explicit feedback to get Priya woven into the narrative where she actually belonged. That's the one thing I had to push on more than I expected.
q5: Yes, I looked at it — and honestly it was pretty well-aligned with what I actually care about. The tension-in-headlines criterion and the integrated social proof criterion especially. What it captured less well is the 'one primary CTA' thing I'm religious about, and the idea that a CTA should feel like the story arriving somewhere rather than a new ask starting. The rubric gestures at that but doesn't quite nail the specificity.
q6: It made me notice that the model is actually quite good at narrative tension from the jump — the 'We thought you should know why' opener was unprompted and exactly right. What the rubric helped me see is that the model's weakness isn't the headline, it's the handoff — getting from the body copy into the CTA without the momentum deflating. That's where it needed the most correction across both tasks.

q3: The CTA pacing was the recurring issue — the model kept resolving tension before asking for the action, which meant the click felt optional instead of inevitable. It took a couple of rounds to get that right, but once I named it, the model fixed it cleanly.
q5: Yes, I looked at it, and honestly it captured a lot of what I care about more precisely than I would have articulated upfront — especially the bit about quotes completing open thoughts rather than echoing resolved ones. That's exactly my instinct but I've never written it down that cleanly. Whether it made the model better is harder to say — I think the model was already moving in the right direction from my feedback, and the rubric may have just reinforced what was already working.
q6: It made me notice that the model is actually pretty good at narrative tension in headlines but tends to deflate it too early in the body — the rubric's framing around 'momentum leading into the CTA never deflating before the reader is asked to act' named a pattern I'd felt in the drafts but hadn't consciously tracked. Seeing it written out made me realize it wasn't a one-off, it was a tendency.
